question_text: A circuit contains two light bulbs. Bulb A has a resistance of 10Ω and Bulb B has a resistance of 15Ω. When connected in series to a 12V battery, Bulb A is much brighter than Bulb B. Explain why this happens and predict what would change if the bulbs were connected in parallel instead.
correct_answer: In series: the same current flows through both bulbs, but Bulb A (lower resistance) has a lower voltage drop across it, so it dissipates less power and is dimmer. Bulb B (higher resistance) has a higher voltage drop and dissipates more power, making it brighter. This is opposite to the question's claim. In parallel: both bulbs experience the same 12V, so Bulb A (lower resistance) draws more current and dissipates more power, becoming brighter than Bulb B.
explanation: In a series circuit, voltage divides across resistors—the higher resistance gets more voltage. In a parallel circuit, voltage is the same across all branches, but lower resistance draws more current and uses more power, producing more brightness.
